summary: Jeff Atwood examines the DiggBar framing controversy and concludes that while Digg's implementation is relatively ethical (opt-in only, uses rel=canonical, no ads), the broader security implications of framing are deeply concerning. He demonstrates that frame-busting JavaScript can be defeated by frame-busting-busting code, creating an unwinnable arms race. The post reveals that due to cross-domain JavaScript security restrictions, there's no reliable way to prevent a malicious site from framing your content. This makes clickjacking attacks—where users unknowingly perform actions on hidden pages—a serious and underappreciated threat that Atwood experienced firsthand on Twitter.
key_points: Digg's framing implementation is relatively ethical: opt-in for logged-in users, uses rel=canonical for SEO, serves no ads, and allows opt-out
Frame-busting JavaScript provides only a false sense of security because it can be defeated by counter-scripts using the onbeforeunload event and 204 HTTP responses
Cross-domain JavaScript security restrictions make it nearly impossible for framed sites to defend against determined attackers
Framing is the gateway to clickjacking attacks, where users perform unintended actions on hidden authentic pages
Websites should implement clickjacking countermeasures assuming they could be framed at any time, regardless of frame-busting code
money_quotes: If an evil website decides it's going to frame your website, you will be framed. Period.
Frame-busting is nothing more than a false sense of security; it doesn't work.
When life serves you lemons, make a lemon cannon.
Don't let frame-busting code lull you into a false sense of security, too.
If the framing site is evil, good luck. When faced with a determined, skilled adversary that wants to frame your content, all bets are off.
The users think that they are clicking the visible buttons, while they are actually performing actions on the hidden page.
I was a skeptic. I didn't want to believe it either. But once shown the exploits on our own site – fortunately, by a white hat security expert – I lived to regret that.
key_insight: Frame-busting JavaScript creates a dangerous false sense of security because determined attackers can always defeat it, making clickjacking countermeasures essential for any website.
